FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline data from 10,842 participants enrolled 2016-2018

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/SM variables: passive screen use (TV/movies/videos), playing video games, social media sites/video chat, texting. All reported separately.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports t-tests and logistic/Poisson regression: screen time duration by autism status (Table 2: p < 0.001, 0.002, 0.001, 0.03, 0.04), obesity prevalence ratios (Tables 3-5: p = 0.002, 0.05, 0.04)

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports p-values, t-statistics, prevalence ratios with 95% CI throughout tables. Examples: boys autism TV 2.9h vs TD 2.3h p < 0.001; obesity/passive viewing p = 0.002 PR 2.15 (1.40-3.31)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using propensity score matching and generalized linear models with Poisson/binomial distributions

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published July 2023 in Frontiers in Pediatrics. No indication of preprint/duplicate status.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes non-SM outcomes (obesity) linked to smartphone/SM variables as predictors. Not inverse direction (SM as DV only). Criterion (a) of FT-EC7 not met.
notes: Paper measures multiple screen time modalities separately (passive, gaming, social, texting) and reports associations with obesity (non-SM outcome). Cross-sectional design at baseline. Autism status is grouping variable, not outcome or predictor.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 8,324 children aged 9-11 years analyzed for neuropsychological associations

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'daily screen time' as specific exposure variable; abstract specifies screen time linked to inhibitory control, reward sensitivity, fronto-striatal connectivity

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports mediation analysis with statistical inference: 'screen time mediated the effect of reward sensitivity on development of inhibitory control system...over two year period' with neuropsychological outcomes (non-SM)

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract describes findings but does not provide explicit β, OR, p-values, or other numeric statistics. Full PDF text needed to confirm presence of numeric coefficients; cannot extract from abstract alone.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: mediation model predicting changes in fronto-striatal connectivity and inhibitory control over two-year period

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published 2023 in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience. No duplicate indication.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes non-SM outcomes (brain connectivity, inhibitory control development). Screen time is IV predicting neuropsychological outcomes, not DV. Does not meet criterion (a).
notes: Longitudinal mediation study (2-year follow-up). Cannot confirm FT_IC4 from abstract alone; numeric values needed from full paper. Screen time is predictor of brain development outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study prospective cohort data: N=11,633 children aged 9-11 years at baseline

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple specific smartphone/SM variables: total screen time, texting, video chatting, watching videos, playing video games. All modalities separately quantified.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports logistic regression: 'Each additional hour of total screen time was prospectively associated with 1.09 higher odds of suicidal behaviors' (aOR 1.09, 95% CI 1.03-1.14); modality-specific ORs for texting, video chat, videos, video games

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports aOR with 95% CIs for multiple associations: total screen aOR 1.09 (1.03-1.14), texting aOR 1.36 (1.06-1.74), video chat aOR 1.30 (1.03-1.65), watching videos aOR 1.21 (1.04-1.39), video games aOR 1.18 (1.01-1.38)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: logistic regression modeling baseline screen time (exposure) predicting suicidal behaviors at 2-year follow-up (outcome)

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published 2023 in Preventive Medicine. No preprint or duplicate indication.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes non-SM outcome (suicidal behaviors/KSADS-5). Screen time is IV predicting clinical/behavioral outcome, not DV. Does not meet criterion (a).
notes: Prospective cohort study with 2-year follow-up. Phone/SM modalities separately extractable as individual IVs. All inclusion criteria clearly met.
